sections.title: 1. What is the Hydrosphere?
points: The Hydrosphere is the scientific name for the part of the Earth made up of water.
Approximately 71% of the Earth's surface is covered by water.
It includes all the world's oceans, seas, rivers, and lakes.
Oceanography: This is the scientific study of all aspects of the ocean.

sections.title: 2. The 5 Major World Oceans
points: Pacific Ocean: The largest and deepest ocean in the world. It covers nearly one-third of the globe.
Atlantic Ocean: The 2nd largest ocean. It is shaped like the letter 'S' and lies between the Americas and Africa/Europe.
Indian Ocean: The 3rd largest ocean. It is known for its warm water and historical trade routes between Africa and Asia.
Southern (Antarctic) Ocean: This ocean completely surrounds the frozen continent of Antarctica at the South Pole.
Arctic Ocean: The smallest, shallowest, and coldest ocean. It is located around the North Pole and is mostly covered in ice.

sections.title: 3. Africa's Surrounding Water Borders
points: Africa is almost entirely surrounded by water, making it nearly an 'island continent'.
To the North: The Mediterranean Sea separates Africa from Europe.
To the North-East: The Red Sea separates Africa from the Arabian Peninsula (Asia).
To the West: The Atlantic Ocean borders the western coast.
To the East: The Indian Ocean borders the eastern coast (East Africa).

sections.title: 4. The Meeting Point: Cape Agulhas
points: The Atlantic Ocean and the Indian Ocean meet at a specific point in South Africa.
This point is called Cape Agulhas.
Cape Agulhas is the most Southern point of the African continent.
South Africa is a unique country because it has coastlines on two different oceans.

sections.title: 5. The Coast and Continental Shelf
points: The Coast: The narrow zone where the land meets the sea or ocean.
Continental Shelf: The shallow part of the ocean floor that borders the continent.
Importance: The continental shelf is vital for the fishing industry because sunlight can reach the bottom, allowing fish food (plankton) to grow.

sections.title: 6. Importance of Africa's Water Borders
points: Trade & Transport: Oceans provide the cheapest way to move heavy goods like oil and machinery using large ships.
Climate: Water from the oceans evaporates to form rainfall which is essential for agriculture in Africa.
Tourism: Coastal regions have beautiful beaches and coral reefs that attract tourists and earn foreign exchange.
Resources: Oceans provide fish for food and are sources of minerals like salt, oil, and gas.

sections.text: PLE Fact: Do not confuse the Mediterranean Sea with an Ocean. It is a Sea because it is smaller and mostly surrounded by land!

cards.fact: The part of the earth covered by water bodies.
The meeting point of the Atlantic and Indian Oceans.
cards.mnemonic: Think of the 'A' in Agulhas for Atlantic meeting point.

cards.fact: The shallow part of the sea near the coast, rich in fish.
cards.mnemonic: Like a kitchen shelf where the food (fish) is kept!

cards.fact: The ocean to the West of Africa, shaped like an S.
